prompt: Complete (taller than Luca): 'Marco è ____ Luca.' (alto)

must_not_include.note: comparing two people against one quality takes di, not che

explanation: 'Marco è più alto di Luca' means 'Marco is taller than Luca.' To compare two different people or things against one quality, Italian builds più + adjective and joins the two with di.

prompt: Complete (less expensive than that one): 'Questo vino è ____ quello.' (caro)

must_not_include.note: two entities, one quality: di, not che

explanation: 'Questo vino è meno caro di quello' means 'This wine is less expensive than that one.' Meno works exactly like più: meno + adjective, then di before the thing compared.

prompt: Complete (bigger than mine): 'Il tuo cane è ____ mio.' (grande)

must_not_include.note: di + il contracts to del; 'di il' is never written

explanation: 'Il tuo cane è più grande del mio' means 'Your dog is bigger than mine.' When di meets a definite article it contracts: di + il = del. The comparison here is against 'il mio (cane)', so di + il becomes del.
examiner_note: Cross-ref: preposition.articulated for the di+article contraction.

prompt: Complete (more than twenty): 'In classe ci sono ____ studenti.' (venti)

must_not_include.note: before a number the comparison uses di, never che

explanation: 'In classe ci sono più di venti studenti' means 'There are more than twenty students in the class.' Before a bare number, 'more than / fewer than' is always più di / meno di, never che.

prompt: Complete (fewer than ten): 'Ho letto ____ libri quest'anno.' (dieci)

must_not_include.note: before a number use di: meno di dieci

explanation: 'Ho letto meno di dieci libri quest'anno' means 'I've read fewer than ten books this year.' The di-before-numbers rule holds for meno too: meno di + number.

prompt: Complete (taller than me): 'Mia sorella è più alta ____.' (io)

must_not_include.note: after di use the stressed pronoun me, not the subject pronoun io
comparing two people: di, not che

explanation: 'Mia sorella è più alta di me' means 'My sister is taller than me.' After di the pronoun takes its stressed form: io becomes me (te, lui, lei, noi, voi, loro).

prompt: Complete: 'È più facile criticare ____.' (agire)

must_not_include.note: two verbs (infinitives) are compared, so the connector is che, not di

explanation: 'È più facile criticare che agire' means 'It's easier to criticise than to act.' When a comparison sets two verbs side by side, the connector is che. Di is only for comparing two different entities against one quality.

prompt: Complete: 'Per me è più divertente giocare ____.' (guardare)

must_not_include.note: two verbs compared: che, not di

explanation: 'Per me è più divertente giocare che guardare' means 'For me, playing is more fun than watching.' Two infinitives in comparison take che.

prompt: Complete: 'Questo attore è più famoso ____.' (bravo)

must_not_include.note: two qualities of the same person are compared, so che

explanation: 'Questo attore è più famoso che bravo' means 'This actor is more famous than talented.' When you weigh two qualities of one and the same thing, the connector is che.

prompt: Complete: 'Questa casa è più comoda ____.' (elegante)

must_not_include.note: two qualities of one thing: che

explanation: 'Questa casa è più comoda che elegante' means 'This house is more comfortable than elegant.' Two adjectives describing the same thing are joined by che.

prompt: Complete: 'In questa città ci sono più turisti ____.' (abitanti)

must_not_include.note: two nouns compared in quantity take che

explanation: 'In questa città ci sono più turisti che abitanti' means 'In this city there are more tourists than inhabitants.' When two nouns are weighed for quantity, the connector is che.

prompt: Complete: 'A colazione bevo più caffè ____.' (tè)

must_not_include.note: two nouns in quantity: che

explanation: 'Bevo più caffè che tè' means 'I drink more coffee than tea.' Two nouns compared for how much take che, not di.

prompt: Complete: 'Vado più spesso a Roma ____.' (a Milano)

must_not_include.note: two prepositional phrases compared take che; keep the preposition a

explanation: 'Vado più spesso a Roma che a Milano' means 'I go to Rome more often than to Milan.' When two prepositional phrases are compared, the connector is che and the preposition (a) is repeated.

prompt: Complete: 'Sono più tranquillo in campagna ____.' (in città)

must_not_include.note: two prepositional phrases: che, with the preposition repeated

explanation: 'Sono più tranquillo in campagna che in città' means 'I'm calmer in the countryside than in the city.' Two phrases introduced by a preposition are joined by che, keeping the preposition (in).

prompt: Complete: 'Marco è più alto ____.' (Luca)

must_not_include.note: two different people compared against one quality take di, not che

explanation: 'Marco è più alto di Luca' means 'Marco is taller than Luca.' Here two separate people are measured against one quality (height), so the connector is di. (Che is only for two qualities, two verbs, or two quantities.)

prompt: Complete: 'Alla festa c'erano più ____.' (venti persone)

must_not_include.note: before a number the comparison uses di

explanation: 'C'erano più di venti persone' means 'There were more than twenty people.' Before a bare number, 'more than' is più di, never più che, even though a quantity is involved.

prompt: Complete: 'Mia figlia è più curiosa ____.' (io)

must_not_include.note: two people compared against one quality take di; after di use the stressed pronoun me

explanation: 'Mia figlia è più curiosa di me' means 'My daughter is more curious than me.' Two people measured against one quality take di, and after di the pronoun is stressed: me, not io.

prompt: Complete: 'A volte è meglio tacere ____.' (parlare)

must_not_include.note: two verbs compared: che

explanation: 'È meglio tacere che parlare' means 'Sometimes it's better to keep quiet than to speak.' Two infinitives weighed against each other take che.

prompt: Complete (as tall as me): 'Mia sorella è alta ____.' (io)

must_not_include.note: after come use the stressed pronoun me, not io

explanation: 'Mia sorella è alta come me' means 'My sister is as tall as me.' Equality is stated with come, and the pronoun after it is stressed: come me (not come io).

prompt: Complete (as much as you): 'Lavora ____.' (tu)

must_not_include.note: after quanto use the stressed pronoun te, not tu

explanation: 'Lavora quanto te' means 'He works as much as you.' Quanto also expresses equality, and it too takes the stressed pronoun: quanto te.

prompt: Complete (as beautiful as it is useful): 'Il libro è ____ utile.' (bello)

must_not_include.note: tanto pairs with quanto, not come
the pair is tanto...quanto (or così...come), not così...quanto

explanation: 'Il libro è tanto bello quanto utile' means 'The book is as beautiful as it is useful.' When both halves are present, the pair is tanto...quanto: tanto + first quality + quanto + second.

prompt: Complete (as serious as it seems): 'La situazione è ____ grave sembra.' (usa: cosi ... come)

must_not_include.note: così pairs with come; tanto pairs with quanto

explanation: 'La situazione è così grave come sembra' means 'The situation is as serious as it seems.' The other equality pair is così...come: così + quality + come.

prompt: Complete (as generous as your father): 'Sei ____ generoso come tuo padre.'

must_not_include.note: with come the first half is così; tanto goes with quanto

explanation: 'Sei così generoso come tuo padre' means 'You're as generous as your father.' When the second half is come, the first half is così, not tanto (tanto belongs with quanto).
examiner_note: Tests the pairing choice only; come is given, learner supplies the matching così.

prompt: Complete (this restaurant is better than that one): 'Questo ristorante ____ di quello.' (buono)

any_phrases.note: correct Italian, but it sidesteps the drilled form: migliore is the inherited comparative under practice here (più buono suits concrete goodness, e.g. tastier)

must_not_include.note: migliore is already a comparative; never più migliore

explanation: 'Questo ristorante è migliore di quello' means 'This restaurant is better than that one.' Buono has an inherited one-word comparative, migliore. The regular più buono survives with a concrete flavour (tastier), so it is accepted here at partial credit; but never più migliore.

prompt: Complete (the traffic today is worse than yesterday): 'Il traffico oggi ____ di ieri.' (cattivo)

any_phrases.note: correct Italian, but it sidesteps the drilled form: peggiore is the inherited comparative under practice here

must_not_include.note: peggiore is already comparative; never più peggiore

explanation: 'Il traffico oggi è peggiore di ieri' means 'The traffic today is worse than yesterday.' Cattivo's inherited comparative is peggiore, alongside the regular più cattivo; never più peggiore.

prompt: Complete (my sister is older than me): 'Mia sorella ____ di me.' (usa la forma di 'grande' per l'età)

must_not_include.note: maggiore is already comparative; never più maggiore

explanation: 'Mia sorella è maggiore di me' means 'My sister is older than me.' For age and rank, grande's inherited comparative maggiore does the work; never più maggiore.

prompt: Complete (the damage is less than expected): 'Per fortuna il danno ____ del previsto.' (usa la forma di 'piccolo')

must_not_include.note: minore is already comparative; never più minore

explanation: 'Il danno è minore del previsto' means 'The damage is less than expected.' Piccolo's inherited comparative minore covers 'lesser, minor, smaller'; never più minore.

prompt: Complete (this pizza is tastier than that one): 'Secondo me questa pizza ____ di quella.' (buono)

markpoints.label: è più buona accepted for concrete goodness

must_not_include.note: never stack più on migliore

explanation: 'Questa pizza è più buona di quella' means 'This pizza is tastier than that one.' For concrete, sensory goodness più buono is fully correct and not a mistake; migliore is also fine. (Più buona agrees with pizza, feminine.) Never più migliore.
examiner_note: Point: più buono must NOT be marked wrong for concrete taste. Both forms at full credit; the copula sits in the answer so 'più migliore' is catchable.

prompt: Complete (better): 'Questo vino è ____ di quello.'

any_phrases.note: very common in speech, but with a noun subject standard Italian wants the adjective migliore

explanation: 'Questo vino è migliore di quello' means 'This wine is better than that one.' Migliore is the adjective and describes a noun (the wine); meglio is the adverb, for how an action is done. English 'better' hides the split, so watch the subject: a thing is migliore.
examiner_note: Ratified ruling: 'è meglio' with a noun subject scores 0.5 with a register note, not a flat miss.

prompt: Complete (better): 'Maria canta ____ di me.'

must_not_include.note: to say how an action is done use the adverb meglio; migliore is the adjective

explanation: 'Maria canta meglio di me' means 'Maria sings better than me.' Here 'better' describes the action of singing, so it is the adverb meglio, not the adjective migliore.

prompt: Complete (better): 'È ____ partire presto.'

must_not_include.note: the impersonal 'it is better to...' uses meglio

explanation: 'È meglio partire presto' means 'It's better to leave early.' The impersonal 'it is better (to do something)' is è meglio, using the adverb.

prompt: Complete (better): 'La tua idea è ____ della mia.'

any_phrases.note: colloquial; with a noun subject (idea) standard Italian wants migliore

explanation: 'La tua idea è migliore della mia' means 'Your idea is better than mine.' The subject is a noun (idea), so 'better' is the adjective migliore.
examiner_note: Ratified ruling: meglio here is 0.5 + register note.

prompt: Complete (better): 'Dopo le vacanze mi sento ____.'

must_not_include.note: sentirsi meglio (to feel better) uses the adverb meglio

explanation: 'Dopo le vacanze mi sento meglio' means 'After the holidays I feel better.' Sentirsi 'to feel' is an action, so it takes the adverb meglio: mi sento meglio.

prompt: Complete (better): 'Il nonno oggi sta ____.'

must_not_include.note: stare meglio (to be/feel better) uses the adverb meglio

explanation: 'Il nonno oggi sta meglio' means 'Grandpa is better today.' Health uses stare, an action verb, so 'better' is the adverb meglio: sta meglio.

prompt: Complete (the best student in the class): 'Marta è la studentessa migliore ____ classe.'

must_not_include.note: the group after a relative superlative takes di (della), not che
di + la contracts to della

explanation: 'Marta è la studentessa migliore della classe' means 'Marta is the best student in the class.' The group a relative superlative picks from is introduced by di (here di + la = della), never by che.

prompt: Complete the whole phrase (the most beautiful city in Italy): 'Firenze è ____ d'Italia.' (città, bella)

markpoints.label: no repeated article after the noun

must_not_include.note: the French-style calque 'la città la più bella'; Italian does not repeat the article after the noun

explanation: 'Firenze è la città più bella d'Italia' means 'Florence is the most beautiful city in Italy.' When the adjective follows the noun, the article appears once: la città più bella, never la città la più bella.
examiner_note: Whole-phrase item: the intervening 'la' in the calque breaks the 'città più' adjacency, so the must_not_include can fire. Agreement (bella) cross-cited to adjective tree.

prompt: Complete (the longest day of the year): 'Il 21 giugno è il giorno ____ dell'anno.' (lungo)
explanation: 'Il 21 giugno è il giorno più lungo dell'anno' means 'June 21st is the longest day of the year.' The relative superlative here is article + noun + più + adjective: il giorno più lungo.

prompt: Complete the whole phrase (the best pizza in the world): 'Molti dicono che Napoli fa ____.' (pizza, buona)

markpoints.label: no repeated article after the noun

must_not_include.note: no repeated article: 'la pizza più buona', not 'la pizza la più buona'

explanation: 'Napoli fa la pizza più buona del mondo' means 'Naples makes the best pizza in the world.' Again a single article: la pizza più buona, with no second article before più.

prompt: Complete (the least expensive of these): 'Tra questi modelli, questo è ____ caro.' (meno)

must_not_include.note: the group takes di/tra, not che

explanation: 'Tra questi modelli, questo è il meno caro' means 'Of these models, this is the least expensive.' The relative superlative with meno keeps the article: il meno caro (tra / di + group).

prompt: Complete (extremely beautiful): 'Il film era ____.' (bello)
explanation: 'Il film era bellissimo' means 'The film was extremely good.' The absolute superlative drops the final vowel and adds -issimo: bello becomes bellissimo.

prompt: Complete (extremely beautiful): 'La vista era ____.' (bello)

must_not_include.note: the -issimo form still agrees; with la vista (f sg) it is bellissima

explanation: 'La vista era bellissima' means 'The view was extremely beautiful.' The -issimo ending agrees like any adjective: with a feminine singular noun it is bellissima.
examiner_note: Agreement is the tested skill here, cross-linked to adjective_agreement.

prompt: Complete (extremely tired): 'Dopo la gara siamo ____.' (stanco)

markpoints.label: spelling: stanco keeps hard c (+ pl agreement)

must_not_include.note: stanco keeps its hard c before -issimo: stanch-issimi

explanation: 'Dopo la gara siamo stanchissimi' means 'After the race we're extremely tired.' Like the plural, stanco keeps its hard c by adding an h: stanchissimi, not stancissimi.

prompt: Complete (extremely tired): 'Sono ____ per il lungo viaggio.' (stanco)

must_not_include.note: keep the hard c with an h: stanchissimo

explanation: 'Sono stanchissimo per il lungo viaggio' means 'I'm extremely tired from the long journey.' Stanco keeps the hard c sound, so the absolute superlative inserts h: stanchissimo.

prompt: Complete (extremely nice): 'Il tuo amico è ____.' (simpatico)

markpoints.label: spelling: simpatico drops to soft c (no h)

must_not_include.note: simpatico takes NO h before -issimo: simpaticissimo

explanation: 'Il tuo amico è simpaticissimo' means 'Your friend is extremely nice.' Unlike stanco, simpatico does not add an h: the superlative is simpaticissimo (mirroring the plural simpatici).
examiner_note: Cross-ref: adjective_agreement.stem_changes. Contrast with cmp_abs_04 (keeps h).

prompt: Complete (extremely long): 'Le vacanze sono state ____.' (lungo)

markpoints.label: spelling: lungo keeps hard g (+ f pl agreement)

must_not_include.note: lungo keeps its hard g with an h: lunghissime

explanation: 'Le vacanze sono state lunghissime' means 'The holidays were extremely long.' Lungo keeps the hard g, adding h before the ending and agreeing feminine plural: lunghissime.

prompt: Say the same thing more mildly than 'bellissimo': 'un panorama ____ bello.' (una parola)

markpoints.label: molto + adj as the milder equivalent

explanation: 'Un panorama molto bello' means 'a very beautiful view.' Molto + adjective says the same as -issimo but more mildly: molto bello is a gentler bellissimo.
examiner_note: Positive test of molto as the mild variant. Stacking 'molto bellissimo' is not engine-catchable (positive substring wins); routed to translation + flagged in coverage.

prompt: Complete (an excellent idea, one word): 'Che ____ idea!' (buono)

any_phrases.note: correct Italian, but it sidesteps the drilled form: ottima is the inherited absolute superlative under practice here

explanation: 'Che ottima idea!' means 'What an excellent idea!' Ottimo is the inherited absolute superlative of buono and is warmer and more idiomatic than buonissimo in most contexts. (Ottima agrees with idea, feminine.)

prompt: Complete (dreadful, one word): 'Il servizio era ____.' (cattivo)

any_phrases.note: correct Italian, but it sidesteps the drilled form: pessimo is the inherited absolute superlative under practice here

explanation: 'Il servizio era pessimo' means 'The service was dreadful.' Pessimo is the inherited absolute superlative of cattivo, the mirror of ottimo.

prompt: Complete (very well): 'Come stai? Sto ____.' (bene)

must_not_include.note: for how you are, use the adverb bene -> benissimo, not buono

explanation: 'Sto benissimo' means 'I'm very well.' How you are is an adverb: bene becomes benissimo (not buonissimo, which is the adjective).

prompt: Complete (very badly): 'Ho dormito ____.' (male)

must_not_include.note: how something goes is an adverb: male -> malissimo, not cattivo

explanation: 'Ho dormito malissimo' means 'I slept very badly.' The adverb male becomes malissimo; cattivissimo would be the adjective and is wrong for how an action goes.

prompt: Complete (his utmost, one word): 'Ha dato il ____.' (usa la forma di 'grande')
explanation: 'Ha dato il massimo' means 'He gave his utmost.' Grande's inherited absolute superlative massimo works as a noun here (il massimo = the maximum), alongside minimo at the other end.
